title: Kundalini and Acceptance: The Practice That Changes Everything
meta_description: You're probably reading this because you're scared. I get it. I've been there. Wide awake at 3 AM, heart pounding, body vibrating like a tuning fork, mind
body:
Kundalini and Acceptance: The Practice That Changes Everything

You're probably reading this because you're scared. I get it. I've been there. Wide awake at 3 AM, heart pounding, body vibrating like a tuning fork, mind racing with questions that have no answers. You're wondering what the hell is happening to you. You've heard the word "kundalini," maybe you even searched for it, and now you're here. You're looking for something, anything, to make sense of the chaos inside you. I'm not gonna lie to you; it's a wild ride. But here's the thing: it doesn't have to break you. It can actually make you. The key? Acceptance.

I remember those nights. Oh god, I remember them. The feeling of being completely unmoored, like your very cells are rearranging themselves without your permission. The bizarre sensations, the fear that you're losing your mind, the desperate search for someone, anyone, who understands. You're not crazy, okay? You're experiencing something very real, something that a lot of people have gone through, often in silence. You're not alone in this. Not even close. I'm telling you this because I needed to hear it then, and I know you do now. Right? That feeling of utter isolation, like you're the only one on the planet experiencing this particular brand of hell - it's a lie. It's the fear talking. Don't listen to it.

What you're going through, whatever its cause - whether it's a spiritual practice gone "wrong," trauma, a spontaneous awakening, or just something your system decided to do - it's intense. And the natural human reaction to intensity, especially when it's uncomfortable or scary, is to resist. To fight it. To push it away. To try and control it. And that, my friend, is where you get into trouble. That's where the suffering really kicks in. Because you can't fight this. You can't control it. It's bigger than you. It's a force of nature moving through you. And the more you resist, the more it pushes back. It's like trying to hold back a tsunami with your bare hands. You're gonna get wiped out.

Acceptance isn't about liking what's happening. It's not about saying, "Oh, this is wonderful! I love feeling like I'm being electrocuted from the inside out!" No, that's not it at all. Acceptance is about acknowledging what is. It's about dropping the fight. It's about saying, "Okay, this is here. This is happening. And I'm not going to add another layer of suffering to it by wishing it wasn't." It's a radical act of surrender. And it's the only way through this without completely losing your mind.

What "Acceptance" Actually Means When Your System is Exploding

Look, when your body feels like it's vibrating apart, or you're having intense energy surges, or you're seeing things, hearing things, feeling things that defy explanation, acceptance sounds like a joke, doesn't it? Like, "Oh, just accept that you're going insane, Kalesh? Great advice!" I know. I know how it sounds. But hear me out. This isn't some airy-fairy, spiritual platitude. This is a survival skill. This is what kept me from going completely over the edge.

Not Resignation, But Acknowledgment

Acceptance isn't resignation. It's not giving up. It's not saying, "I'm doomed." It's saying, "This is my reality right now." It's recognizing that the pain, the fear, the discomfort - a huge chunk of it comes from your resistance to what's happening. Think about it. You feel a weird sensation. Your mind immediately jumps to "What's wrong with me? Am I dying? Is this permanent?" That thought creates more fear. That fear creates more tension in your body. That tension makes the original sensation even worse. It's a vicious cycle, isn't it? You know what I mean?

What if, instead, when that weird sensation popped up, you just noticed it? "Oh, there's that buzzing feeling again." No judgment. No story. Just observation. It's like watching clouds pass in the sky. They're there. You see them. You don't try to stop them. You don't try to change their shape. You just observe. This takes practice, a lot of it, especially when the clouds are made of lightning and fire. But it's possible. And it makes a difference you wouldn't believe.

The Power of "And"

One of the most powerful tools I learned was the word "and." Instead of "I'm scared but I need to be strong," try "I'm scared and I'm strong." Or "This energy is overwhelming and I'm breathing through it." It allows two seemingly contradictory truths to coexist. It stops the internal battle. You're not trying to push away the fear to get to the strength. You're holding both. You're acknowledging both. That's acceptance in action. It's not about eradicating the unpleasant feelings; it's about making space for them. It's about allowing them to be there without letting them consume you. You're not the feelings. You're the one experiencing them. Big difference.

I found a lot of help in books that talked about this idea, even if they weren't specifically about kundalini. For instance, The Mindfulness and Acceptance Workbook for Anxiety (paid link) really helped me understand how to apply these principles to the sheer terror I was feeling. And then there's Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach (paid link), which, honestly, felt like it was written for me during those dark times. It wasn't just about accepting the external situation, but accepting the inner turmoil, the self-judgment, the pain. That's a huge piece of it. We beat ourselves up for feeling what we feel, and that just makes everything worse.

Why Resistance Makes It Worse

You're probably experiencing a lot of physical and mental discomfort. Let's be real, some of it is downright terrifying. But here's the thing: a huge portion of your suffering isn't from the sensations themselves, but from your reaction to them. Your mind's insistence that "this shouldn't be happening." Your frantic attempts to make it stop. Your judgment of yourself for not being "normal."

When you resist, you create tension. Physical tension, mental tension, emotional tension. This tension acts like a dam, blocking the natural flow of energy. And guess what happens when you block a powerful force? It builds up. It creates pressure. It looks for other ways to move, often in more erratic and uncomfortable ways. So those weird sensations? They get amplified. The fear? It gets magnified. The anxiety? It spirals. It's like trying to hold your breath underwater. The urge to breathe only gets stronger, more desperate, more painful, the longer you resist.

I've seen this happen countless times, both in myself and in others I've talked to who've been through this. People try to suppress the energy, to meditate it away, to pray it away, to ignore it. And it just comes back stronger, usually with a vengeance. It's like a child throwing a tantrum - the more you tell them to stop, the louder they get. You've gotta change your approach. You can't control the tantrum, but you can control your reaction to it.

The Illusion of Control

One of the hardest lessons to learn is that you're not in control here. And for a lot of us, that's a terrifying realization. We spend our whole lives trying to control our environment, our bodies, our thoughts, our emotions. We believe that if we just try hard enough, we can bend reality to our will. But kundalini, or whatever this energy is, laughs at that idea. It strips away that illusion of control, often brutally. And that's okay. Because it's not about losing control; it's about learning a different kind of control - the control over your reaction, over your internal state, regardless of what's happening externally or internally. That's where your real power lies.

So yeah, you're not in charge of the energy. But you are in charge of how you relate to it. That's your superpower right now. That's the practice. That's the only thing that truly changes everything.

Practical Steps for Acceptance When You're Freaking Out

Okay, so you get it intellectually. "Acceptance, got it." But how the hell do you actually do it when your body is convulsing or your mind is screaming? It's not easy. It's a moment-to-moment practice. It's not a one-time decision. Here's what helped me, and what I've seen help others:

  • Name It to Tame It: When a sensation or emotion arises, label it without judgment. "Fear." "Buzzing." "Heat." "Anxiety." Just the word. Don't add a story to it. Don't say, "Oh god, this buzzing means I'm going to die." Just "buzzing." This creates a tiny bit of space between you and the experience.
  • Breathe Into It: When you feel resistance, or tension, or fear, direct your breath to that area. Imagine your breath creating space around the sensation. You're not trying to push it away with your breath; you're inviting it to be there, creating room for it. It sounds simple, but it's incredibly powerful.
  • "This Too Shall Pass": Repeat this to yourself. Over and over again. Because it's true. Nothing is permanent. Every sensation, every emotion, every thought - it all comes and goes. Reminding yourself of this can help you ride the waves instead of fighting them.
  • Radical Self-Compassion: You're going through something incredibly difficult. Be kind to yourself. You wouldn't yell at a child who's scared or in pain, would you? So don't yell at yourself. Talk to yourself like you would your best friend who's struggling. "It's okay to feel this. You're doing your best. I'm here with you."
  • Grounding Practices: Even while accepting the internal storm, you need to feel connected to reality. Simple things like feeling your feet on the floor, noticing five things you can see, four things you can hear, three things you can touch, two things you can smell, and one thing you can taste. This pulls you out of the internal maelstrom and into the present moment, which is always safer than the narratives your mind creates.
  • Seek Support (the right kind): This is huge. You need people who won't dismiss what you're experiencing. You need people who understand that this isn't just "stress" or "anxiety" in the conventional sense. I found immense comfort in reading accounts from others who had similar experiences. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) was a lifesaver for me, just knowing I wasn't alone in the bizarre symptoms. And The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) gave me a approach that helped me understand what was happening without feeling like I was losing my mind.

You're not trying to fix anything. You're trying to allow. You're trying to flow. It's like being in a river. You can fight the current and exhaust yourself, or you can turn your body and let it carry you. The river's still moving, but your experience of it is entirely different. Does that land?

The Long Game: Integration, Not Eradication

This isn't a quick fix. This isn't a "poof, it's gone" kind of situation. This is a process. A deep, messy, sometimes terrifying, sometimes exhilarating process of transformation. And acceptance is your constant companion on that journey. You're not trying to get rid of the energy; you're learning to live with it, to integrate it, to let it reshape you. Because that's what it's doing. It's breaking down old structures, old beliefs, old patterns that no longer serve you. And that's why it's so uncomfortable. It's like having your house renovated while you're still living in it. It's noisy, dusty, and inconvenient. But the end result can be beautiful.

So yeah, it's a long game. There will be good days and bad days. Days where you feel like you've got this, and days where you're back on the floor, crying, wondering if it'll ever end. That's normal. That's part of it. Don't judge yourself for those moments. Just come back to acceptance. Come back to the breath. Come back to just noticing. Again and again and again. That's the practice. That's the work. And it's worth it. I promise you, it's worth it.

You're stronger than you think. You've got this. Just breathe, and accept, and allow. One moment at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
